tiberius_longinus_convo_0_n01.npcDialogue: Do shut that, would you? Wonderful. I've grown particular about doors since the Lex Varia — half my dining companions are currently explaining themselves to tribunals, and the other half cross the street when they see me. Funny how that works. But you — you came looking, which means someone told you old Longinus still has his uses. They weren't wrong, friend. The only question worth asking is what you've brought to trade.

options.playerDialogue: I didn't bring a purse, Longinus. I brought a mutual problem — which, if I'm not mistaken, is worth considerably more.
What's it cost? I've got a short afternoon and a shorter patience for theatre.
I hear you kept very warm relations with certain towns south of the Liris. I'm not a Varian prosecutor — I think warm relations have their uses.

tiberius_longinus_convo_0_n02.npcDialogue: Partnership — lovely word. Very Greek. In my experience it means the smaller fish does the swimming while the bigger fish does the eating. But you're not entirely wrong, friend. Everyone in Rome who hasn't nailed his colours to a faction is being pressed like olives — the optimates want oaths, the populares want bodies for their legions, and the Italians want our heads on stakes. The middle ground is getting rather narrow, and rather red.

options.playerDialogue: Unless the man in the middle knows which way to step before everyone else does. That's your particular talent, I'm told.

tiberius_longinus_convo_0_n03.npcDialogue: Oh, I do enjoy the blunt ones — so restful after a day of euphemisms. My price isn't silver, friend. I've got estates for that. What I need is — let's call it atmospheric management. The Lex Varia tribunals are developing an appetite for men who traded with Italian towns before all this unpleasantness. Someone needs to mention to the right magistrate that Tiberius Longinus's grain contracts were commerce, not conspiracy. Preferably before they mention me at all.

options.playerDialogue: I can whisper. But I'll need to know the whisper is worth the breath.

tiberius_longinus_convo_0_n04.npcDialogue: Careful, friend. That particular observation got a man named Caepio a very short walk to a very deep river last month. But yes — I had arrangements with certain Italian towns. Grain, iron, the odd bolt of Apulian wool. Perfectly dull commerce. Only now those same routes carry rather more interesting cargo than textiles — if one knows how to listen to the mule-drivers. The question is what sort of man you are. The sort who serves the Republic, or the sort who serves himself?

options.playerDialogue: In my experience, the best intelligence is like good wine — it improves every hand it passes through.
The Republic's, naturally. I'm a Cornelian — we bleed for the city, we don't bill her.

tiberius_longinus_convo_0_n05.npcDialogue: Then here's your dividend, friend — free of charge, call it a sample. The Italian confederacy is held together with spit and spite. The Marsi and the Samnites agree on exactly one thing: they'd like us dead. Beyond that? Poppaedius Silo wants a toga and a vote — he wants in. The Samnites want us gone from Campania entirely — they want out. That's not an alliance, that's a marriage on its last night. The crack is there. The question is whether you know where to set the chisel.

responses.playerDialogue: Offer the toga to every Italian who sheathes his sword. The holdouts face the legions alone — clean, lawful, and absolutely merciless.
Talk to the Marsi. Quietly, separately. Give Poppaedius what he actually wants, and let the Samnites wake up alone.
Put a rumour in the Samnite camp that the Marsi are already negotiating a separate peace. Let panic do the work a legion can't.
Sell this to the optimates and the populares both — separately, of course. Let them bid against each other for the privilege of being clever. We take the margin.

fallbackResponse.playerDialogue: I'd want to sleep on that. Possibly twice. Back rooms are for making deals, not strategy.

tiberius_longinus_convo_0_n06.npcDialogue: Measured. Careful. A man after my own heart — assuming I have one. Let me think about how this arrangement might serve us both. I do love a good arrangement.

tiberius_longinus_convo_0_n07.npcDialogue: Now that — that has a certain music to it, friend. Since we're being harmonious, let me share something specific. There's a grain shipment sitting in a warehouse in Puteoli — my warehouse, as it happens — addressed to parties who are currently trying to kill Roman soldiers. I can send it to our garrison instead, or I can let it walk south to the Samnites. Each road has its tolls. Which would you recommend to a man who values your counsel?

options.playerDialogue: Send it to our garrison. Hungry Samnites make short wars.
Let it walk — but make damned sure Poppaedius knows who let it walk. That's not grain, that's a favour. And favours compound.

tiberius_longinus_convo_0_n08.npcDialogue: Done. The grain pivots north by morning. I'll see to it the right names reach the right ears — yours among them, naturally. You know, friend, I keep two sets of books: one for debts, one for allies. As of tonight, you're in both. I do hope you'll find that more comfortable than it sounds.

tiberius_longinus_convo_0_n09.npcDialogue: A gambler. Wonderful — gamblers are the only honest men left in Rome; at least they admit they're guessing. The grain goes south, and when Poppaedius opens that shipment he'll find a little note explaining who kept it moving. We'll both have a chit with the Italians that may be worth more than a province when the swords stop. But do remember, friend — I arranged it. You merely advised. That distinction is the kind of thing that keeps a man breathing.

tiberius_longinus_convo_0_n10.npcDialogue: Well. A patrol caught the shipment at the harbour mouth — your stratagem, my warehouse, and now a garrison commander with an inconvenient fondness for questions. Don't worry, friend. I'll manage it. I always manage it. That's what I do. But you should know — you now owe me something rather significant, and I am a patient collector. I'll come calling when the moment ripens. Do try to look surprised when I do.

operations.reason: Redirected Italian supplies to Roman forces
The grain never arrived — the Italians suspect treachery

deltas.reason: Chose Roman interests over personal profit

operations.reason: Facilitated supplies to the Italian confederacy
Suspicions arise about your Italian sympathies

deltas.reason: Built leverage across enemy lines through commerce

operations.reason: The grain seizure is seen as loyalty to Rome

deltas.reason: Attempted a scheme that went sideways — but learned from it

effects.reason: Cleaning up the mess from the botched grain deal costs you time
